retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Membrane -- Cellular Level -- Membrane Composition
retrieved_docs.content: At the basic level, the cell membrane is a collection of lipids (namely phospholipid) and protein components. The phospholipid structure forms from a hydrophilic phosphorylated glycerol "head" and two hydrophobic fatty acid "tails." The cell membrane possesses a phospholipid bilayer, with the hydrophilic heads facing outwards and the hydrophobic tails facing inwards. [1] An additional lipid component is cholesterol, which prevents excessive membrane fluidity at elevated temperatures and ironically allows for membrane fluidity at decreased temperatures by preventing lipids from packing together. [2] The protein component of the cell membrane includes either integral proteins (embedded within the membrane) and peripheral proteins (outside of the membrane).  Examples of essential proteins include any membrane channels, membrane pores, membrane receptors, and adhesion anchors. [3] Peripheral proteins loosely attach to the outside of the cell membrane by electrostatic forces; these peripheral proteins serve multiple functions in the cell membrane, including signaling, recognition, membrane trafficking, cell division, and cell structure. [4] The lipid component (phospholipids, cholesterol, and glycolipids) allows for the high permeability of lipid-soluble compounds such as steroid hormones, carbon dioxide, and oxygen. These lipids also inhibit the passive diffusion of hydrophilic substances. The protein component is responsible for transporting hydrophilic substances such as water, glucose, and ions.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Cell Membrane
retrieved_docs.content: The cell membrane, also known as the plasma membrane or plasmalemma, derives its name from the Greek word " lemma, " which means bark. This trilaminar structure is composed primarily of lipids, including phospholipids, cholesterol, and glycolipids. Cell membrane proteins include integral membrane proteins, peripheral membrane proteins, and glycoproteins. A small amount of carbohydrates is also present in this protective barrier.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Microscopy, Light -- Cell Membrane
retrieved_docs.content: The cell membrane measures only 7.5 nm. Consequently, this structure may be observed only under an electron microscope. The cell membrane serves as a barrier that holds the cell together, separates it from the surrounding environment, and maintains its shape. Membrane rupture results in the release of cellular contents.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Clinical Significance -- Cell Membrane Defects
retrieved_docs.content: Membrane defects are also implicated in receptor-related disorders like Graves disease, certain types of diabetes and obesity, and multiple sclerosis. Changes in lipid composition, as seen in multiple sclerosis and muscular dystrophy, further disrupt membrane function. Abnormal membrane permeability plays a role in cystic fibrosis, bacterial toxin-related conditions, and diseases linked to specific transport alterations. Additionally, cytoskeleton-membrane defects, such as those in Chediak-Higashi disease, further illustrate the diverse clinical impact of membrane dysfunction. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Membrane -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The human cell membrane is the cornerstone of an elaborate interplay between the extracellular and the intracellular worlds. Understanding the physiology of the cell membrane provides the foundation for understanding many processes in the human body, from the mechanism of the heart beating to how neurons communicate, arrhythmias evolve, and muscle pathology in many neurological diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Extracellular Vesicles -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The vesicles are surrounded by phospholipid bilayer membrane similar to the one that surrounds the cells. [6] However, lipidemic and proteomic studies showed that the membrane of the vesicles has its natural lipid and protein profile. One of the unique features of this profile, for example, is the high level of the lysoglycerophospholipid, which is absent in the other cellular membranes. [7] They also reach in cholesterol, ceramides, and sphingomyelin. Regarding the protein content of the vesicle membrane, it shows heterogeneity not only between EVs isolated from different cells but even between EVs isolated from the same type of cells under various conditions. However, some proteins are common in different EVs like tetraspanins and integrins families, which are used as markers for characterization of the isolated EVs. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Cell -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Each cell is enclosed by a delicate plasma membrane that separates its internal contents from the external environment. Within the cell, the protoplasm consists of both the cytoplasm and the nucleus (see Image . Ring Sideroblasts). The cytoplasm is composed of the cytosol, also known as hyaloplasm, and various organelles. The cell membrane functions as a selective barrier that maintains cellular integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Membrane -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids are an essential component of the cell membrane. The structure is typically made of a glycerol backbone, 2 fatty acid tails (hydrophobic), and a phosphate group (hydrophilic). As such, phospholipids are amphipathic. In the cell membrane, phospholipids are arranged in a bilayer manner, providing cell protection and serving as a barrier to certain molecules. The hydrophilic part faces outward and the hydrophobic part faces inward. This arrangement helps monitor which molecules can enter and exit the cell. For example, nonpolar molecules and small polar molecules, such as oxygen and water, can easily diffuse in and out of the cell. Large, polar molecules, for example, glucose, cannot pass freely so they need the help of transport proteins.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Epithelial Cell -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The basal domain is connected to the basal lamina by hemidesmosomes, which combine with intermediate filaments. The basal lamina separates connective tissue from the epithelium. The lateral domain connects neighboring cells and allows for communication between cells. There are a variety of junctional complexes that connect adjacent cells. Desmosomes anchor/adhere junctions that tightly join cells by integrating with the cytoskeletal structures. Tight junctions are occluding junctions that regulate the movement of fluid and solutes. Gap junctions are communicating junctions found throughout the lateral domain, creating channels that allow small molecules and ions to pass between adjacent cells. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Parietal Cells -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Considered to be a highly specialized epithelial cell, [4] the parietal cell has an intricate structure that allows it to fulfill its complex duties. Pyramidal in shape, parietal cell cytoplasm is packed with mitochondria, with abundant lysosomes and a specialized organelle, tubulovesicles. [8] The apical membrane contains secretory canaliculi; these canaliculi project into the cell’s interior and communicate with each other. [4] These cells have two different structural stages, the resting and secreting states. In the resting state, the apical canaliculi are lined with microvilli; actin microfilaments and actin-binding proteins support them. [4] The resting parietal cell's apical membrane has a limited area, but a more prominent luminal exposure is achieved by the invagination of this membrane into the cytoplasm, thus forming the secretory canaliculus, in its collapsed state. [8] Contrastingly, during its secreting state, the tubulovesicular compartment appears to be depleted due to the fusion and transfer of tubulovesicular membranes to the apical membrane. [2] This activity also results in the elongation of intra-canalicular microvilli. [8] These tubulovesicles are found to develop interconnections during the presence of a stimulus, and the gastric acid pump gets transported from these tubulovesicles towards the apical membrane, thus culminating in the acid-secretory state. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Water Balance -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: The intracellular fluid is comprised of at least 10 separate minuscule cellular packages. For simplicity and to make the analysis of the intracellular space viable, the concept of a united intracellular “compartment” has been created. These collections have important unifying similarities such as location, composition, and behavior, which provide practical utility in studying physiology. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Membrane -- Function -- Electrochemical Gradient and Resting Membrane Potential
retrieved_docs.content: The Na/K pump uses ATP to transport three Na ions extracellularly and two K ions intracellularly. This process causes the pooling of K intracellularly and Na extracellularly. [6] Based on natural diffusion, molecules will always travel from a high concentration to a low concentration (chemical gradient). The cell membrane contains many K "leak" channels, which allow the high intracellular K to flow down its chemical gradient and deposit extracellularly. These channels are always open and thus always "leaking" potassium out of the cell. Potassium ions have a positive charge. When K ions move from inside to the outside of the cell via its chemical gradient, these positive ions deposit on the outside of the membrane, creating transmembrane electrical potential with a positive charge outside of the membrane and negative charge on the inside of the membrane; this transmembrane potential creates the electrical gradient of the cell membrane in which anything with a positive charge will attempt to flow down its electrical gradient and move intracellularly. In theory, ions will stop moving across the cell membrane when the ion's chemical gradient is equal to its electrical gradient; at this point, the cell membrane is at its resting membrane potential. The cell membrane has varying levels of permeability to Na, K, and chloride (Cl), with K being the most permeable in the resting state, and thus, K has the most influence on the resting membrane potential of the cell. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipopolysaccharide -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The gram-negative bacterial cell membrane is composed of an outer membrane and an inner membrane. The outer membrane is exposed to the outside environment, while the inner membrane envelops the cytoplasm. Contrary to the usual biological membrane, the gram-negative bacterial membrane does not have a phospholipid bilayer but an asymmetric bilayer with LPS on the outside and phospholipid on the inside. [4] In non-capsulated bacterial strains, such as E.coli , Pseudomonas , etc., LPS is exposed to the cell surface, while in capsulated strains, such as Klebsiella pneumonia , Haemophilus influenza , etc., LPS is present below the capsular layer.

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Epithelial Cell -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Epithelial cells are located throughout the body and have many functions based on morphology and location. Structures of the apical domain significantly affect function. Microvilli are involved in fluid transport and absorption. The number of microvilli correlates to the absorptive properties of the cells. Also, cilia transport substances across the surface of epithelial cells. Stereocilia are essential in hearing and balance. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Histology, Red Blood Cell -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The mature erythrocyte has a biconcave, discoid shape and is anucleated. [1] This design allows for the flexibility needed to navigate the cardiovascular system and for an increased surface area which supports sufficient gas exchange and permits the cell to carry out its function. A phospholipid bilayer membrane frames the structure of this unique cell and is maintained by a network of proteins that make up the cytoskeleton. This cytoskeleton is composed of spectrin, actin, band 3, protein 4.1, and ankyrin—which allows for cellular structural integrity as well as malleability. The interactions between these compounds support a structurally sound yet pliable structure. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1287
retrieved_docs.content: or ancillary benefits rather than primary pharmacologic effect. From a mechanistic perspective, vitamin A exerts physiological effects classified as “Cellular Activity Alteration,” influencing cell differentiation, growth, and immune function. Its mechanism of action includes specific enzyme interactions that modulate retinoid signaling pathways. Structurally, vitamin A belongs to the retinol acetate class, underscoring its chemical relationship to retinoids. Additionally, vitamin A is related to antioxidant activity, contributing to the neutralization of free radicals and protection of cellular membranes. Collectively, these relationships illustrate that vitamin A serves multiple roles across pharmaceutical and nutraceutical products: as a vital nutrient in multivitamin regimens, as a component of complex botanical and mineral blends, as an excipient in protein‑based or topical formulations, and as a biologically active retinoid with cellular, enzymatic, and antioxidant functions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: B [Preparations] are pharmaceutical or nutraceutical formulations whose parent compound is beta‑tocopherol, a specific isoform of vitamin E. Beta‑tocopherol functions as a potent antioxidant, protecting cellular membranes from oxidative damage and supporting various physiological processes. By using beta‑tocopherol as the foundational ingredient, B [Preparations] leverage its antioxidant properties to enhance therapeutic efficacy, improve stability of active ingredients, and contribute to overall patient health.

The term “antioxidancia” (antioxidant) is linked to vitamin A, highlighting its capacity to neutralize free radicals and protect cellular membranes from oxidative damage. Structurally, vitamin A belongs to the retinol acetate class, confirming its chemical identity as a retinoid derivative. Its physiologic impact includes cellular activity alteration, and it is recognized for its antioxidant capacity (“antioxidancia”), contributing to the protection of cellular membranes from oxidative stress. The structural classification of vitamin A as retinol acetate aligns with its role in the visual cycle, immune competence, and epithelial maintenance. Collectively, the extensive presence of vitamin A across oral supplements, multivitamins, prenatal formulations, and certain topical agents reflects its essential role in human health. Its inclusion supports nutritional adequacy, antioxidant defense, and specific physiological processes, while its mechanistic actions via enzyme interactions and cellular modulation provide a scientific basis for its therapeutic and preventive applications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1579

Regulatory frameworks designate many of these agents as approved qualifiers for procurement, storage and distribution within medical and pharmaceutical channels, ensuring lawful availability for patient care. Integration of toxicity profiles, pharmacokinetic dynamics and regulatory status enables clinicians to tailor dosing regimens, anticipate drug interactions and enhance overall patient safety across this diverse set of therapeutic, nutritional, hormonal and biochemical agents.

Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that vitamin A is integrated across a spectrum of therapeutic and nutritional products—ranging from sublingual solutions and oral multivitamins to topical skin‑care preparations—where it functions either as a primary active agent or as a supportive inactive component. Its biological actions, including modulation of cellular activity, enzyme interaction, and antioxidant protection, underpin its widespread inclusion in formulations aimed at supporting vision, immune health, skin integrity, and overall micronutrient adequacy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is classified as a fat‑soluble vitamin, meaning it belongs to the broader group of vitamins that dissolve in lipids and require dietary fat for optimal absorption. This places vitamin K within the same category as vitamins A, D, and E, highlighting shared characteristics such as storage in fatty tissues and the need for adequate fat intake to ensure proper bioavailability. Recognizing vitamin K as a fat‑soluble vitamin is essential for understanding its absorption dynamics, its role in physiological processes like blood coagulation, and for guiding nutritional recommendations that support its effectiveness.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 806
retrieved_docs.content: not serve the primary therapeutic purpose. Beyond its presence in formulations, vitamin A exerts a physiological effect described as “Cellular Activity Alteration,” indicating its influence on cell differentiation, growth, and immune function. The compound is also linked to antioxidant activity (“antioxidancia”), underscoring its capacity to neutralize free radicals and protect cellular membranes. Structurally, vitamin A belongs to the retinol acetate class, reflecting its chemical derivation from retinol esterified with acetic acid. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate that vitamin A functions both as a principal active agent in many vitamin supplements and as a supporting or inactive component in certain topical and specialty products, while also contributing essential cellular and antioxidant functions that are central to its therapeutic and nutritional value.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 154

Finally, vitamin A (plain) and vitamin D (and analogues) are grouped under “vitamin A and D, including combinations of the two,” while vitamin B₁ (plain) belongs to the class “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂,” reflecting the availability of both single‑nutrient and combination products. This structured representation integrates nutrient taxonomy, mineral‑vitamin interactions, deficiency states, and related pharmacologic agents, providing a comprehensive framework for patient nutrition and therapeutic management.
